subject: ap-psychology
theme: The biological bases of behavior encompass the structure and function of neurons, neural communication, major brain regions, the two hemispheres, and how genetics and evolution shape psychological traits.
core_concepts: Neurons transmit signals via the action potential — an all-or-none electrical impulse triggered when depolarization exceeds threshold. Neurotransmitters are released at the synapse and bind to receptors on the postsynaptic neuron, producing excitatory or inhibitory effects.

The central nervous system (brain + spinal cord) integrates information; the peripheral nervous system (somatic + autonomic) connects the CNS to the body. The autonomic system's sympathetic division mobilizes the fight-or-flight response; the parasympathetic division restores rest-and-digest.

Major brain structures: the brainstem (medulla, pons, reticular formation) controls survival functions and arousal; the cerebellum coordinates movement and procedural memory; the limbic system (hippocampus = memory consolidation, amygdala = fear/emotion, hypothalamus = drives) regulates motivation and emotion; the cerebral cortex handles higher cognition.

Brain lateralization: left hemisphere governs language (Broca's area = speech production; Wernicke's area = language comprehension) and analytic processing; right hemisphere handles spatial tasks, facial recognition, and holistic processing. The corpus callosum connects and coordinates the two hemispheres.

Brain plasticity means the nervous system reorganizes through experience — synaptic pruning, neurogenesis (hippocampus), and functional reorganization after injury. Plasticity is greatest in early development but continues throughout life.

Behavioral genetics studies heredity's role using twin studies (MZ vs DZ comparisons), adoption studies, and heritability estimates. Most psychological traits reflect gene-environment interaction — genes set reaction ranges that environments shape.

Key neurotransmitter systems: dopamine (reward, movement — deficient in Parkinson's, excess linked to schizophrenia), serotonin (mood, sleep — targeted by SSRIs), GABA (inhibitory — targeted by benzodiazepines), acetylcholine (muscle control, memory — blocked in Alzheimer's), norepinephrine (arousal, fight-or-flight).
exam_tip: FRQ case studies require naming a specific brain structure or neurotransmitter AND explaining its function — partial credit is lost if you only name the structure without explaining the mechanism.
